ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for black chickpea chaat; closest parent is chickpea food product (FOODON_03311737).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for black chickpea chaat; A00LC (legume-based prepared dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for black chickpea chaat or sufficiently close legume chaat concept.

preparation_method: served cold or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of black chickpea chaat is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at an Indian street food stall or restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Bengal gram (black, whole, raw) and composite chaat preparation estimates
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for cooked whole black gram / Bengal gram (kala chana). Values for the composite chaat dish are adapted from the cooked legume base (approximately 60% of dish weight) combined with fresh vegetable components (onion, tomato, coriander ~30%) and chutney/spice dressing (~10%), weighted proportionally. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate are derived from this weighted composition. Sodium is elevated relative to plain cooked chickpeas due to added salt, chaat masala, and tamarind chutney.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — chickpeas (Bengal gram, boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 28 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled chickpeas (Bengal gram / kala chana), which is the primary ingredient of black chickpea chaat. The composite chaat dish includes acidic components (tamarind, lemon juice) and high-fibre vegetables that are expected to maintain or lower the GI relative to plain boiled chickpeas. GI of 28 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate for the composite dish. GL per serving = (28 × 22.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 12.6 g per 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt, chaat masala, and tamarind chutney
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving provides approximately 620 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt and chutney portions, and account for this dish within a daily sodium target of under 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 28) and high dietary fibre from black chickpeas
type2_diabetes.guidance: Black chickpea chaat has a low GI of approximately 28 and provides around 11.6 g dietary fibre per serving, supporting postprandial glucose control; it is a suitable legume-based option for individuals managing type 2 diabetes when portion size is observed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High dietary fibre, low saturated fat, and cholesterol-free legume base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Black chickpea chaat is cholesterol-free, low in saturated fat (0.4 g per 100 g), and rich in soluble fibre, which supports LDL cholesterol reduction; the moderate sodium content from chutneys and spices should be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black chickpea chaat (kala chana chaat) is a popular Indian street food and snack dish made primarily from boiled black chickpeas (kala chana / Bengal gram), dressed with finely chopped onion, tomato, green chilli, and fresh coriander, then seasoned with chaat masala, roasted cumin powder, black salt, lemon juice, tamarind chutney, and green mint-coriander chutney. The dish is served cold or at room temperature and is characterised by a bold combination of tangy, spicy, and savoury flavours with a firm yet tender chickpea texture. Black chickpeas are nutritionally denser than white chickpeas, offering higher iron, fibre, and antioxidant content. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 142 kcal, 7.2 g protein, 2.8 g fat, 22.5 g carbohydrate, and 5.8 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is low (approximately 28), making it a suitable option for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Sodium content is moderate due to added salt, chaat masala, and chutneys, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension. Commonly served as a street food snack, light lunch, or appetiser across India and in Indian diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in black channa chaat?
Is kala chana chaat good for diabetics?
What is the GI of chana chaat?

What are the ingredients in black channa chaat?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked black chickpeas (kala chana) with proportional adjustment for the composite chaat dish components. No exact ICMR-NIN entry exists for the composite black chickpea chaat dish as served in restaurants. GI is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled Bengal gram and applied as an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than high because the restaurant-served dish (Dugout, Circular Road, Singapore) may vary in ingredient ratios, chutney quantities, and added salt.
notes: Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. Vitamin D is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no fortification; confirmed zero by formulation. Trans fat is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in this preparation; confirmed zero by formulation. Cholesterol is 0.0 as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation. The cuisine_tag 'Butter Chicken' in the input record appears to be a field survey platform tagging artefact and has been disregarded as it is inconsistent with the dish identity. Nutrient values represent a typical composite preparation; actual restaurant values may vary. GI of 28 is sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled Bengal gram (kala chana) and applied as an analogous estimate for the composite chaat dish.
